writing-text-into-files-try-it-1.title: Print the morning briefing
writing-text-into-files-try-it-1.prompt: Print the line `Morning briefing` in the terminal with `echo`.
writing-text-into-files-try-it-1.hint: Use the terminal. Check your work with `pwd`, `ls`, `cat`, or `find` before clicking Run.

help.hint_1: Create folders before putting files inside them.
help.hint_2: When you are unsure, inspect the folder first, then make the next small change.

echo-prints-text.title: `echo` prints text
echo-prints-text.bodyMarkdown:
`echo` prints text in the terminal.

```bash
echo "Club checklist"
```

That line appears as output. By itself, `echo` does not save the text in a file.

write-with-greater-than.title: Use `>` to write or replace a file
write-with-greater-than.bodyMarkdown:
The `>` symbol sends output into a file.

```bash
echo "Club checklist" > checklist.txt
cat checklist.txt
```

Be careful: `>` replaces the file content if the file already has text.

append-with-double-greater-than.title: Use `>>` to add another line
append-with-double-greater-than.bodyMarkdown:
The `>>` symbol appends to the end of a file.

```bash
echo "Bring markers" >> checklist.txt
cat checklist.txt
```

Use `>>` when you want to keep the existing content and add one more line.
